---
title: "Outcome-Wide Analysis"
---

```{=html}
<img src="images/outcome-wide-analysis-logo.png"
     alt="Outcome-Wide Analysis logo"
     style="
       float: right;
       width: 180px;
       height: auto;
       margin: 0 0 14px 22px;
     ">
```

Outcome-Wide Analysis provides consulting support for researchers and public agencies working at the intersection of policy, environment, transportation, and population health.

I help teams develop rigorous, policy-relevant studies, particularly where causal inference, spatial data, and public-health concepts intersect.

## Areas of expertise

* Quasi-experimental methods for policy evaluation, including difference-in-differences, generalized synthetic control, and related approaches
* Epidemiologic study design and analytic strategy, including innovative case-control designs for transportation-safety and place-based research
* Framing transportation and environmental questions within population-health and public-health frameworks
* Integration of complex datasets, including spatial, mobility, environmental, and administrative data sources
* Scientific writing support for grants and manuscripts, particularly methods and conceptual framing sections

## Background

Michael Garber, PhD, MPH is an epidemiologist whose work focuses on how transportation systems, urban design, and environmental policy shape population health. He has authored 25+ peer-reviewed articles, including work published in *Epidemiology*, *The Lancet Planetary Health*, and *Journal of Transport & Health*, and received the 2022 Rothman Epidemiology Prize for methodological innovation.

## Contact
To discuss a possible project, please email: `michael@outcomewide.com`


## On the name
The name *Outcome-Wide Analysis* is inspired by Tyler VanderWeele’s 2017 article "Outcome-wide Epidemiology,” which argues for studying multiple outcomes togteher rather than one at a time. This thinking informs my approach to studying upstream determinants of population health, including transportation, environmental, and land-use decisions that can affect health, climate, equity, mobility, and quality of life through interconnected pathways.
